translations.reference: As part of efforts to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience the UAE, WHO, and partners are working hand-in-hand to deliver supplies to reach the world’s most vulnerable in their time of greatest need.” For his part, Dr Ahmed Al-Mandhari, WHO Regional Director for the Eastern Mediterranean, said, “The World Health Organization continues to coordinate with the Sudanese health authorities to deliver essential health supplies through all available pathways. Due to the conflict, hospitals, pharmacies and primary health care facilities have stocked out of essential, life-saving medicines. The arrival of an additional 30 metric tons of trauma and emergency surgery supplies as well as essential medicines will enable WHO to support 13 major health facilities and re-establish health care services for those in need. Our global logistics hub in Dubai plays a vital role in ensuring an uninterrupted supply of health commodities and we are grateful for the support provided by the United Arab Emirates to facilitate our first charter flight of these desperately needed supplies. It is support such as this that shows in action our Regional vision of health for all, by all.” The medical aid provided by the World Health Organization through the UAE accounted for approximately 55% of the total medical supplies provided to Sudan from abroad in 2022, reflecting the UAE's position as a major hub for international humanitarian assistance.

The arrival of large quantities – over 30 million – of essential supplies, including emergency cleaning supplies and vital medicines, will allow WHO to support 13 major health facilities and restore healthcare services to those in need. Logistics hub in Dubai plays a crucial role in ensuring the uninterrupted delivery of health supplies, and we are grateful for the support provided by the United Arab Emirates to facilitate the first shipment of supplies urgently needed by the affected population. This is a commitment as it demonstrates the vision of our Health Region that everyone is valued and supported. The assistance provided by the World Health Organization to the United Arab Emirates to Sudan reached 55% of the total number of health supplies delivered in 2022, highlighting the UAE’s position as a major humanitarian aid hub in other countries.

translations.source: Ukubambisana endaweni kubalulekile ekuqhubekeni emhlabeni okuya kokuhlobene nezempilo zamaSDG Umbiko wokuqhubeka omusha ukhishwe ngokuhlanganyela namhlanje kuma ejensi asayinile angu-13 eGlobal Action Plan for Helath Lives and Well being for All (SDG3 GAP). Umbiko usihloko esthi "What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?" uma kubhekwa iminyaka emine kusetshenzwa ndawonye ngama-ejensi ezindlela eziningi ezinkulu kuvezwa ukuthi yikuphi okusebenzile nokungasebenzanga ekuqinisweni ukubambisana nokwesekwa kwenqubeko yezwe okuuya nokuhlobene nezempilo zeSustainable Development Goals (amaSDG). Lo nyaka umaka iphuzu lahhafu eliya kumaSDG kodwa umhlaba usele ngemuva ukufika kuzinhloso zomhlaba. Kusukela ngokwethulwa kwayo ngo-2019, iSDG3 GAP yenze ukubambisana okusha kwezinhlaka phakathi kokusayina kwama ejensi ezindaweni ezibalulekile njengokugcineka kwezimali nokunakekelwa kwezempilo kokuqala kwabanye abantu. Okungenani amazwe angu-67 akhulumile kanye noma ngaphezulu ngeSDG3 GAP ukuqhuba izingqikithi. Senze inqubeko ebalulekile, kodwa kusanendlela ende okumele siyihambe ukwenza ngcono indlela izinhlangano eziningi ezisebenzisana ngayo ukweseka amazwe. Kumele silalele esikutshelwa ngamazwe futhi silandele imiyalelo,kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO noSihlalo weSDG3 GAP kuzinhloko zeqoqo. "Ngibonga ababambisani ngokubambisana kwazo nokuzihlola ngokwethembeka okuqukethwe kulombiko. "Umbiko unikeza iziphakamiso eziyisithupha ezibalulekile ukuqinisekisa ukuthi amazwe ayazuza ekwesekweni okukhishiwe nokubona ukuzibophezela okwenziwe kuSDG3 GAP.
translations.reference: Collaborating locally is key for progress globally towards health-related SDGs A new progress report is being jointly released today by the 13 signatory agencies of the Global Action Plan for Healthy Lives and Well-being for All (SDG3 GAP). The report entitled “What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?” reflects four years of joint work by major multilateral agencies outlining what has and has not worked in strengthening collaboration and support to accelerate country progress towards health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). This year marks the halfway point toward the SDGs, but the world is lagging behind to reach the global goals. Since its launch in 2019, the SDG3 GAP enabled creation of new collaboration structures between the signatory agencies in key areas such as sustainable financing and primary health care among others. At least 67 countries engaged in on one or more of the SDG3 GAP accelerator themes. "We have made important progress, but we still have a long path to travel to improve the way that multilateral organizations work together to support countries. We must listen to what countries tell us and act upon their guidance,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General and Chair of the SDG3 GAP Principals Group. “I thank the partners for their collaboration and for the honest self-assessments contained in this report. "The report provides six key recommendations to ensure that countries benefit from a more streamlined support and to realize the commitments made in the SDG3 GAP.

translations.source: Imiphumela yocwaningo olusha ikhombisa izinhlelo zempilo ziqala ukuba ngcono esikhathini sobhubhane Ngemva kweminyaka emithathu yobhubhane lweCOVID-19 izinhlelo zempilo emazweni seziqale ukukhombisa izinkomba ezinkulu zokuvuseleleka kwezinhlelo zempilo, ngokweWHO umbiko wesikhashana emzuliswaneni wesine wocwaningo lephalisi yomhlaba ekuqhubekeni kwemisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile ngesikhathi sobhubhane lweCOVID-19: Lwezi 2022-Masingana 2023. Ngokuqala kuka-2023 amazwe abike ukwenzeka kokwehla kokuphazamiseka ekulethweni kwendlela yemisebenzi yezempilo,kodwa kwavezwa isidingo sokutshala kokungalungiswa nokubekezela kakhulu kwikusasa. Kulamazwe angu-139 aphendule kumzuliswane wesine wokuzwakala kocwaningo lweWHO, amazwe abike ukuqhubeka kokuphazamiseka emisebenzini yekota esilinganisweni. Emazweni angu-84 lapho ukuhlaziya okudumile kungenzeka,iphesenti lemisebenzi ephazamisekile yehlile ngesilinganiso esisuka ku-56% ngoNtulikazi kuya kuMandulo 2020 ngo-23 % ngoLwezi 2022-Masingana 2023. Ukuqhubeka kokuphazamiseka kungenxa yakho kokubili ukufuneka nokusaplaya izinkinga eziseceleni, okufaka amazinga aphansi okunakekelwa kwezempilo emphakathini okudingayo kanye nomkhawulo wokubakhona kwabasebenzi bezempilo nezinsizakusebenza ezinye zokunakekelwa kwezempilo njengokuvulwa kwemitholampilo noma ukubakhona kwesitoko semithi nemikhiqizo. "Izindaba ezamukelwayo ukuthi izinhlelozempilo emzaweni amaningi ziqala ukuvuselela imisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile ezigidini zabantu abawugejile ngesikhathi sobhubhane," kusho uDkt Rudi Eggers, Umqondisi weWHO weMisebenzi yeZempilo yoKuhlanganisiwe. "Kodwa sidinga ukuqinisekisa ukuthi wonke amazwe ayaqhubeka nokuvala izikhala ukuvuselela imisebenzi yezempilo,nokusebenzisa izifundo abazifundile ukwakha izinhlelo zempilo ezizilungiselele nokubekezela kwekusasa". Izimpawu zokuqala eziqaphelekayo ekuvuseleleni Kulolu cwaningo olusha,amazwe ambalwa abike ngokungenhloso isikalo sasemuva sokuthola kuzinkundla zonke umsebenzi wokuletha nokusebenza kwezempilo yomphakathi okubalulekile kusukela 2020-2021 ukubikwa kukhombisa ukubaluleka kwegxathu kubuyelwe emazingeni angaphambi kobhubhane ukulethwa kokusebenza nokusebenza okukhulu kwezinhlelo.
translations.reference: New survey results show health systems starting to recover from pandemic After three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, health systems in countries have started showing the first major signs of health system recovery, according to the WHO interim report on the “Fourth round of the global pulse survey on continuity of essential health services during the COVID-19 pandemic: November 2022–January 2023”. By early 2023, countries reported experiencing reduced disruptions in the delivery of routine health services, but highlighted the need to invest in recovery and stronger resilience for the future. Among the 139 countries that responded to the fourth round of WHO’s pulse survey, countries reported continued disruptions in almost one-quarter of services on average. In 84 countries where trend analysis is possible, the percentage of disrupted services declined on average from 56% in July-September 2020 to 23% in November 2022- January 2023. Persisting disruptions are due to both demand- and supply-side factors, including low levels of health care-seeking in communities as well as limited availability of health workers and other health-care resources such as open clinics or available stocks of medicines and products. “It is welcome news that health systems in the majority of countries are starting to restore essential health services for millions of people who missed them during the pandemic,” said Dr Rudi Eggers, WHO Director for Integrated Health Services. “But we need to ensure that all countries continue to close this gap to recover health services, and apply lessons learnt to build more prepared and resilient health systems for the future”. First notable signs of recovery In this new survey, fewer countries reported intentionally scaling back access across all service delivery platforms and essential public health functions since 2020-2021 reporting, showing an important step to return to pre-pandemic levels of service delivery and broader system functioning.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English.gs:

**New research findings indicate that health systems are beginning to improve during the pandemic.**

Following three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, health systems in countries have begun to show significant signs of recovery, according to a WHO interim report in its fourth round of global health research survey on the continuation of essential health services during the COVID-19 pandemic: for 2022-2023.  

Starting in 2023, countries reported a decrease in disruptions to the delivery of essential health services, but the need for significant correction and future preparedness remains.  Among the 139 countries that responded to the fourth round of WHO health research survey, countries reported continued disruptions in the workforce. In 84 countries where significant recovery was possible, the percentage of disrupted services decreased from an average of 56% from July to December 2020 to 23% by 2022-2023.

The continued disruption is due to both the need for workforce replenishment and the lack of adequate staffing levels in the community health sector, as well as limitations in the availability of health workers and other support for health care, such as the closure of hospitals or the shortage of medicines and medical supplies. “Positive news is emerging that health systems in many countries are beginning to update essential health services provided to vulnerable populations during the pandemic,” said Dr. Rudi Eggers, WHO Director of Integrated Health Services. “But we need to ensure that all countries continue to prioritize the update of essential health services and utilize the lessons learned to build resilient and future-ready health systems.”

Initial key indicators observed in this new study, several countries reported a deliberate shift to a new model of community essential health services delivery from 2020-2021, indicating the importance of this approach has returned to pre-pandemic levels of delivery and significant operation of systems.

translations.source: Ekupheleni kuka-2022, amazwe amaningi abike izimpawu ezingacacile zokuvuselelwa kokusebenza okufaka ukusebenza kwezocansi, ukuzala, abakhullwe, abazelwe, izingane nezempilo yentsha, ukudla okunempilo ukugomma,izifo ezithelelanayo (okufaka umalaleveva, iHIV, iTB nezinye izifo ezithelelana ngokocansi) izifo ezidumile ezinganakiwe,izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo,ukuphathwa kwengqondo, okwengqondo nesifo sokusebenzisa izidakamiza, ukunakekelwa kwabantu abadala nokunakekelwa okulekelelayo nokwesiko. Inombolo yamazwe abika ukuphazamiseka ohlelweni lwawo lesaplayi sheni ezweni okungafika kuhhafu (29 ka-59 wamazwe aphendulayo) okunganekota (18 wa-66 wamazwe) aphedulayo kulonyaka odlule. Ngaphandle kwezinkomba zokuvuselela,ukuphazamiseka komsebenzi kuyaqhubeka emazweni onke namazinga emali engenayo kuzona zonke izinhlelo soomsebenzi wokulatha nokulandelela umsebenzi ezindaweni. Amazwe aphinde abhekane nokunyuka komsebenzi okumele wenziwe - ikakhulukazi emisebenzini yokubheka, ukuxilonga nokwelapha izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo - okungaholela kumiphumela engemihle uma abantu bebambezeleka ukuthola ukunakekelwa ngesikhathi. Ukuvuselelwa kwemisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile kubucayi ngenxa yokuphazamiseka - okufaka imisebenzi yokugqugquzela ezempilo, ukuvinjwa kwezifo, ukwelapha, ukuvuselelwa kwesimilo nephaliyeshini - kungaba nokukhulu umthelela ohlukene wezempil nezinga lomuntu ukunobhubhane qobo ikakhulukazi kubantu babuthaka. Ukuhlanganisa imisebenzi yeCOVID-19 emisebenzini yezempilo ebalulekile elinye igxathu elibalulekile eliya ekuvuselelweni kohlelo noguquko,amazwe amaningi asebe nenqubekela phambili ekuhlanganiseni imisebenzi yeCOVID-19 endleleni yokuletha imisebenzi yezempilo. Cishe u-80%-90% wamazwe ahlanganyele ngokuphelele ekugonyweni kweCOVID-19 ukuxilonga nemisebenzi yokuphathwa kwecala kanye nemisebenzi yesimo sangemuva kweCOVID-19 endleleni yomsebenzi wokuletha. Kuyafana amazwe amaningi (u-80% wamazwe awu-83 aphendulile) abike okungenani okuncane ekukhuphukeni kokuthola amathuluzi abalulekile eCovid-19 (isibonelo ukuxilongwa kweCOVID-19, ukwelaphangxube, imigomo namathuluzi okuzivikela siqu - PPE), ngezinkinga zokusebenza kwezempilo nokwentuleka koxhasomali okumele izivimbo ezijwayeleke kakhulu. Ukuqhubeka kokusekwa kuyadingeka ukuvuselela,ukubekezela nokuzilungiselela Amazwe amaningi aseqalile asebenzisa asebekufundile ngesikhathi sobhubhane lweCOVID-19 okufaka ngezikhungo zenamba yokusbenza kokusha ukuphazamisa amasu endleleni yokulethwa kwezempilo.
translations.reference: By the end of 2022, most countries reported partial signs of service recovery, including in services for sexual, reproductive, maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health; nutrition; immunization; communicable diseases (including malaria, HIV, TB, and other sexually-transmitted infections); neglected tropical diseases; noncommunicable diseases; management of mental, neurological and substance use disorders; care for older people; and traditional and/or complementary care. The number of countries reporting disruption to their national supply chain system reduced from nearly half (29 of 59 responding countries) to about a quarter (18 of 66 responding countries) within the last year. Despite signs of recovery, service disruptions persist across countries in all regions and income levels, and across most service delivery settings and tracer service areas. Countries are also dealing with increasing service backlogs – most frequently in services for screening, diagnosis and treatment of noncommunicable diseases – which can lead to negative consequences as people are delayed access to timely care. Recovering essential health service delivery is critical because disruptions – including to services for health promotion, disease prevention, diagnosis, treatment, rehabilitation and palliation – may have even greater adverse health effects at population and individual level than the pandemic itself, especially among vulnerable populations. Integrating COVID-19 services into essential health services In another important step towards system recovery and transition, most countries have made progress in integrating COVID-19 services into routine health service delivery. About 80-90% of countries have fully integrated COVID-19 vaccination, diagnostic and case management services as well as services for post COVID-19 condition into routine service delivery. Still, most countries (80% of 83 responding countries) reported at least one bottleneck to scaling up access to essential COVID-19 tools (e.g. COVID-19 diagnostics, therapeutics, vaccines and personal protective equipment - PPE), with health workforce issues and lack of funding representing the most common barriers. Further support needed for recovery, resilience and preparedness Most countries have started to apply what they have learnt during the COVID-19 pandemic, including through the institutionalization of a number of innovative service disruption mitigation strategies into routine health service delivery.

**Translation:**

At the end of 2022, many countries have reported clear indicators of renewed efforts to improve health and well-being, including access to healthcare for pregnant women, newborns, the elderly, people with disabilities, children, and overall public health. This includes access to nutritious food, essential medicines, chronic diseases (such as malaria, HIV, TB, and other infectious diseases), preventable diseases, diseases that cannot be treated, mental health services, substance abuse treatment, elderly care and essential social support. Approximately half (29-59%) of responding countries (18-66%) reported disruptions to their health systems last year.

In addition to the indicators of renewed effort, workforce disruptions continue in all countries and low-income countries across all employment programs. Countries are also facing an increasing need for workforce – particularly in areas such as surveillance, investigation, and treatment of non-communicable diseases – which can lead to negative outcomes if people are delayed in receiving care.  The prioritization of essential health functions is being impacted by disruptions – including health promotion, disease prevention, treatment, immunization, and health facility revitalization – which can have a significant impact on maternal and child health and the overall well-being of populations, particularly vulnerable groups.

Integrating COVID-19-related tasks into essential health functions is a crucial element of the renewed effort, and many countries are leading the way in incorporating COVID-19 tasks in this manner. Approximately 80-90% of countries are fully involved in COVID-19 surveillance and case management tasks and post-COVID-19 status assessments. Many countries (80% of 83 respondents) also report a relatively small increase in the availability of key COVID-19 tools (such as COVID-19 testing, vaccines, treatment protocols, and personal protective equipment – PPE), alongside challenges related to workforce shortages and funding constraints that require significant attention. Continued support is needed to update, scale up, and prepare. Many countries that were early adopters of lessons learned during the COVID-19 pandemic, including the establishment of new operational support hubs, are disrupting existing pathways to health service delivery.

translations.source: Lokhu kufaka ukubekwa kwezindlela zokwelapha ngefoni,ukugqugquzela ukunakekelwa usekhaya nokungenelela kokuzinakekela, izindlela zokuqinisa ukubakhona kwabasebenzi bezempilo, amandla nezindlela zokweseka, ukwenza kabusha ekuthengeni nokuletha imithi namasaplayi ezinye izindlela zokuxhumana nomphakathi ukubambisana nabanikezeli bomkhakha ozimele nowomphakathi. Amakota amathathu ezwe abaike ukufakwa koxhasomali olwengeziwe oluya ekuvuselelweni kohlelo lwesikhathi eside, ukubekezela nokuzilungiselela. Amazwe agcizelele isidingo sokwesekwa kweWHO ekubhekaneni nezinkinga ezisele ngokwengqikithi yeCOVID-19 nangale,ikakhulukazi okujwayele ukuhlobana nokuqinisa ukusebenza kwezempilo, ukwakha amandla okubheka imisebenzi yezempilo, ukwakha ukunakekelwa kwezempilo kokuqala okuncike kuzindlela zokunakekela, ukubusa, inqubomgomo nokuhlela nokuhlela ezezimali noxhasomali. Inothi kubahleli: Emzuliswaneni wesine wocwaningo lwephasile yomhlaba weWHO, amazwe angu-222, izindawo bamenywa ukuba baphendule kucwaningo oluncike kuwebhu eqinisikesiwe phakathi kukaLwezi 2022 noMasingana 2023. Ucwaningo lwesine lulandele kucwaningo lwephasile edlule yango-2020 no2021 lweWHO: uMzuliswano 1 (Nhlaba-Mandulo 2020) uMzuliswano 2 (Masingana-Ndasa 2021) noMzuliswano 3 (Lwezi-Zibandlela 2021) okwakhombisa ukukhulu bokuthi ubhubhane lalukhahlameza ukuqhubeka kwemisebenzi yezempilo ebalulekile nokuthi amazwe enza kanjani. Ngesikhathi ucwaningo lwephasile lunemikhawulo njengokubika ukuchema nokumela,amandla alomzamo ukuthi mukhulu futhi uletha ulwazi ngokuphindekile. Itemu "izwe" kumele liqondwe ukuthi lifaka wonke amazwe, imingcele nezindawo. Abahlaziya okudumile kwaqedwa kuncike ezimpendulweni eziphuma emazweni angu-84, abahlukumezi noma izindawo ezaphendula okungenani kusahluko socwaningo olulodwa kuyona yonke imizuliswano emine yocwaningo.
translations.reference: These include deployment of telemedicine approaches, promotion of home-based care or self-care interventions, approaches for strengthening health workers availability, capacities and support mechanisms, innovations in procuring and delivering medicines and supplies, more routine community communications, and partnerships with private sector providers. Three quarters of countries reported additional funding allocation towards longer term system recovery, resilience and preparedness. Countries expressed need for WHO support to address remaining challenges in the COVID-19 context and beyond, most frequently related to health workforce strengthening, building the monitoring capacities of health services, designing primary health care -- oriented models of care, governance, policy and planning and financial planning and funding. Note to editors: In the fourth round of WHO’s global pulse survey, 222 countries, territories and areas were invited to respond to a standardized web-based survey between November 2022 and January 2023. The fourth survey followed up on WHO’s previous 2020 and 2021 pulse surveys: Round 1 (May-September 2020), Round 2 (January-March 2021), and Round 3 (November-December 2021) which showed the extent to which the pandemic was affecting the continuity of essential health services and how countries are taking action. While pulse surveys have limitations such as reporting bias and representativeness, the strength of this effort is that it is comprehensive and delivers information rapidly. The term “country” should be understood to include all countries, territories and areas. Trend analyses was completed based on responses from 84 countries, territories, or areas that responded to at least one survey section in all four pulse survey rounds.

This document outlines the implementation of telephone-based healthcare services, emphasizing home care and self-care, strengthening the capacity of healthcare professionals, essential resources, and alternative methods of communication.  It also includes efforts to rebuild and bring back trees and other sustainable resources, and to enhance community communication and collaboration with relevant national and community stakeholders. 

There are three key areas of focus: securing additional funding for long-term support and preparedness, planning and preparing for it.  Countries have indicated the need for WHO support in addressing the remaining challenges related to the COVID-19 pandemic and related issues, particularly regarding the continuity and strengthening of healthcare services, building capacity for health interventions, establishing initial healthcare support reliant on care delivery, governance, and financial planning and resource mobilization. 

A key finding from the fourth phase of the WHO global survey is that 222 countries were invited to participate in a web-based survey between July 2022 and September 2023. This survey followed the third phase of the 2020 (Nhlaba-Mandulo) and 2021 (Masingana-Ndasa) surveys and the second phase (Lwezi-Zibandlela 2021), which highlighted the surprising continuation of essential healthcare activities and how countries responded.  Despite limitations such as self-reporting bias, the survey provided extensive information. The term "country" should be understood to encompass all countries, borders, and regions.  Data analysis was conducted based on responses from 84 countries, with varying levels of responsiveness to each of the four survey phases.

translations.source: Isitatimende sokubungaza ukuphela kweminyaka engu-50 yeHRP: iSweden ne-United Kingdom of Great Britain neNorthen Ireland Ukubamba isimo seNqubomgomo neKomidi lokuXhumana (PCC) Amalungu abungaza ukuphela kuka-50 HRP nokuzuza okuningi, iSweden neUnited Kingdom of Great Britain neNorthern Ireland ukufunda ukuqapheleka okulandelayo kokwamukela nokuhalalisa emhlanganweni wesi-36 weHRP PCC. Isizukulwane sethu sesibone inqubekela phambili enkulu kusayensi nezobuchwepheshe ukuthi konke okuqhubekayo kwesizukulwane ndawonye, nokubona okwemfundo okumbalwa,ezobuchwepheshe, ezokwelapha noguquko lwenhlalakahle luqhutshwa ukuguquka kokuvikela kulandelwa ukwenzangcono kwezempilo yokuzala, ukwenza kabusha kwezempilo kanye namalungelo nokulingana ngokobulili. Kepha ukucabanga izingqinamba emkhakheni weSRHR uqhubeke futhi kuphikiswane,okunye ukuqhubeka kusembuzweni futhi namhlanje. Sincoma abasebenzi beHRP ababambisani nabeseki kwabakutholile okuningi kuze kube yinamhlanje nokubona izinto ezinkulu ezenzekile ngesikhathi esinguhhafu wekhulunyaka ekukhiqizeni ubufakazi obeseka ukungena okuphelele nokulinganayo kwezempilo namalungelo ezocansi nokuzala. Singathanda ukuveza imikhakha emikhulu emithathu yokuzuziwe Okokuqala ucwaningo olusezingeni eliphezulu lweHRP lube nomthelela obonakalayo kunqubomgomo yezempilo yomphakathi,amazinga, nokwenza emhlabeni wonke okubhekene nezidingo zokuguquka kusibalo sabantu esahlukene. Okwesibili HRP iqapheleke ngeHRP Alliance, isiqinise izikhungo zocwaningpo endaweni nasemhlabeni,kwavumela abacwaningi abaningi ukuthi bathole amakhono okucwabinga ukuqinisekisa ubunhalo obuphezulu bocwaningo lweSRHR ukuthi liqhubeke futhi lunyuke esikhathini esizayo. Okwesithathu,umhlaba uhlelwa yizinkinga, ukuxabana nokunyusa ukuqaba ekulinganeni kobulili, iHRP isize ukubamba isisekelo samalungelo abasefazane nezingane nabantu ababuthaka. I-HRP ihlala iyithuluzi elibalulekile kuhlelo lweUnited Nations yocwaningo lokhukhiqiza kubantu nokuqhubeka nokuba umholi ovelele kwezempilo yezocansi nokuzala emhlabeni.
translations.reference: Statement of celebration for HRP’s 50th anniversary: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland To capture the spirit of Policy and Coordination Committee (PCC) members celebrating HRP’s 50th anniversary and its numerous achievements, Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland read the following note of appreciation and congratulations at the 36th meeting of the HRP PCC. Our generation has seen more progress in science and technology than all preceding generations together and witnessed several intellectual, technological, medical and social transformation spearheaded by the contraceptive revolution and followed by advances in reproductive health, health innovations as well as rights and gender equality. However, considerable challenges in the field of SRHR continue, and, arguably, some advances are in question again today. We applaud HRP-staff, partners and supporters on the numerous achievements to date and recognise the enormous strides accomplished during half a century in producing the evidence that supports full and equal access to sexual and reproductive health and rights. We would like to outline three major categories of achievements: Firstly, the high-quality research by HRP has had tangible impact on public health policy, standards, and practice worldwide addressing the evolving needs of diverse populations. Secondly, HRP, notably through the HRP Alliance, has strengthened research institutions locally and globally, and, enabled numerous researchers to acquire the research skills that ensure that high-quality SRHR research can continue, and indeed increase, into the future. Thirdly, in a world beset by crisis, conflict and increasing resistance to gender equality, HRP has facilitated the upholding of the basic rights of women and girls and vulnerable populations. HRP remains the main instrument within the United Nations system for research in human reproduction and continues to be an exceptional leader in global sexual and reproductive health.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English.gs:

**Statement Regarding the 50-Year Milestone of the HRP: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland**

This document addresses the framework and the Communication Committee (PCC) regarding the 50th anniversary of the HRP (Reproductive Health Programme) and future developments. Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland will be reviewing the following considerations upon accepting and implementing at the 36th HRP PCC meeting.

Our commitment to science and expertise continues to advance, with ongoing efforts in education, specialized skills, healthcare, and overall well-being, driven by a focus on improving reproductive health, restoring health and rights, and promoting gender equality. However, considering the challenges within the SRHR (Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights) sector, continued thought and debate remain, and this is particularly relevant today. We recommend that HRP staff continue to collaborate with stakeholders who have achieved significant progress in providing comprehensive and equitable access to health and rights, particularly in reproductive health. We would like to highlight three key areas of focus. Firstly, high-level research on the HRP should be prioritized within the framework of community health policy, at national and global levels, addressing diverse needs for change. Secondly, the HRP recognizes the HRP Alliance, supporting research organizations globally, enabling numerous researchers to gain expertise to ensure rigorous SRHR research continues and expands in the future. Thirdly, the world faces issues of inequality, violence, and discrimination in relation to gender equality; the HRP supports the upholding of women’s and children’s rights. The HRP remains a vital tool within the United Nations system for research focused on the well-being of women and reproductive health globally.

translations.source: I-WHO yethula umzamo omusha wokwenza ngcono ukuzilungiselela kobhubhane Ukusiza amazwe ukuthi azilungisele okungcono kwekusasa lobhubhane,WHO yethule umzamo namahlanje ozonikeza imiyalelo yokuhlela ngokuhlanganisa ngokuphendula kunoma iyiphi iphathojini yokuphefumula njengomkhuhlane noma ikhorona. I-Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats Initiative, noma iPRET, ihlanganisa amathuluzi akamuva nezindlela zokufunda okwabelwana ngazo kanye nesenzo esiyiqoqo esisungulwe phakathi nobhadane lweCOVID-19 nezinye izimo eziphuthumayo zakamuva zezempilo zomphakathi. Ngomzamo iWHO izosebenzisa indlela yokudlulisela ukuyala amazwe ekuhleleni ubhubhane,ngoba kukhona amandla amaningi namandla amaningi afanayo kumaqoqo ephathojini. I-PRET iphendula isimemezelo somuyalelo wezobuchwepheshe nokwesekwa kokugqugquzelwa nokuqiniswa kokuhlanganisa ukuzilungiselela nokuphendula,njengova kuveziwe kuzinqumo zeWorld Health Assembly. Ububhane lweCOVID-19 nezinye izimo eziphuthumayo zezempilo zikhombisile ukuthi amazwe adinga ukuthi alungele ukubhekana nezinsongo zezifo ezithathelwanayo, ngezinhlelo zokuzilungiselela ezilungiselelwe ngesandla kanye nokuxhumana okungcono nokusebenzisana neminye imikhakha efana nezolimo. "Ukuzilungiselela, ukuvikela nemisebenzi yokuphendula akumele kube yisifundazwe somkhakha wezempilo kuphela," kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO. "Njengeziphuthumayo zempilo zinomthelela kumikhakha eminingi, ngakho nokuzilungiselela kwethu nemizamo yokuphendula mayilingane nemikhakah, izinhlobo namagciwane. Kubucayi futhi ukuthi ukukhuluma komphakathi nokulingana kuphakathi kwemizamo yethu, ikakhulukazi kulabo bantu abagcindezelekile futhi abasengcupheni enkulu." I-PRET iwuguquko endleleni yeWHO ekuzilungiseleni kobhubhane ngokusebenzisa indlela yelensi yokudlulisela, ukunokugxila ezifweni ezithile.
translations.reference: WHO launches new initiative to improve pandemic preparedness To help countries better prepare for future pandemics, WHO launched a new initiative today that provides guidance on integrated planning for responding to any respiratory pathogen such as influenza or coronaviruses. The new Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats Initiative, or PRET, incorporates the latest tools and approaches for shared learning and collective action established during the COVID-19 pandemic and other recent public health emergencies. Through the initiative, WHO will use a mode of transmission approach to guide countries in pandemic planning, given that many capacities and capabilities are common among groups of pathogens. PRET answers the call for technical guidance and support for promoting and strengthening integrated preparedness and response, as outlined in World Health Assembly resolutions. The COVID-19 pandemic and other health emergencies have shown that countries need to be operationally ready to respond to infectious disease threats, with tailored preparedness plans in hand and better coordination and collaboration with other sectors such as agriculture. “Preparedness, prevention, and response activities must not be the province of the health sector alone,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “Just as health emergencies have impacts across many sectors, so must our preparedness and response efforts span sectors, disciplines and pathogens. It is critical, too, that community engagement and equity are the centre of our efforts, especially for those populations that are marginalized and most at risk.” PRET is an evolution in WHO’s approach to pandemic preparedness through the application of a mode of transmission lens, rather than a focus on specific diseases.

**WHO Launches New Initiative to Better Prepare for Pandemics**

To help countries better prepare for a future pandemic, WHO has launched a new initiative aimed at providing guidance on preparedness, including responding to emerging respiratory pathogens such as influenza or coronaviruses. The “Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats Initiative,” or PRET, includes the latest tools and methods for shared learning and a collaborative action framework established during the COVID-19 pandemic and recent other recent public health emergencies. Through this initiative, WHO will utilize a coordinated approach to support countries in pandemic preparedness, as there are many similar strengths and capacities across pandemic response teams.

PRET responds to a call for expertise and support to strengthen and validate integrated preparedness and response efforts, as has been explicitly noted in World Health Assembly decisions. The COVID-19 pandemic and other recent public health emergencies have shown that countries need to be ready to address the challenges of infectious diseases, with tailored preparedness plans and improved communication and collaboration with other sectors such as agriculture. “Preparedness, protection and response activities should not be the sole responsibility of the health sector,” said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “As health emergencies span multiple sectors, preparedness and response efforts must be coordinated across sectors, including animal and human health. It is also crucial that community engagement and knowledge sharing are integral to our efforts, particularly for vulnerable populations who are disproportionately affected.”

PRET represents a shift in WHO’s pandemic preparedness approach by utilizing a scaling-up lens, with a focus on specific threats. 


---

Ukwethulwa komzamo kuzokwenzeka ngosuku lokugcina lwemihlangano abangaphezu kuka-120 abamele Member State, ababambisani, noNobhala weWHO, ukuchaza indlela eya phambili yePRET okufaka ukuthuthuka kwebalazwe lokwenza lomhlaba kokuzilungiselela lobhubhane lwegciwane lokuphefumula. Imizamo yePRET isifundo sokuqala sigxile kumagciwane pokuphefumula, okufaka umkhuhlane, igciwane lekhorona namagciwane ezokuphefumula. Ngokunikezwa ubhubhane oluqhubekayo nokwesabisa okungenzeka emkhuhlaneni, lemojuli izokwenza amazwe abheke ngokucophelela, ukuhlola, ukubuyekeza imizamo yokuhlela ubhubhane lwezokuphefumula ukuqinisekisa banamandla okusebenzayo, namandla akhona. Uhlelo lusendleleni lokuhlonza iqoqo elilandelayo lamagciwane, njengama-arbovirus, ukuthi kubhekwane nawo ngaphansi kwalomzamo. Lokhu kuzolandela okuhamba phambili okuhlonziwe ngeziphakamiso eziyishumi zokuqinisa ukwakhiwa komhlaba kokuzilungiselela kokuphuthuma kwezempilo, ukuphendula nokubekezela (HEPR). Umzamo wePRET ukuyisa esikhathini esisha sokuzilungiselela kobhubhane nokumela uguquko lwemisebenzi ngqo yeWHO ukweseka wonke amaMember State ekuqiniseni ezempilo zokuzilungiselela okuphuthumayo, ukuvikela namandla okuphendula nokukwazi. Umzamo ungaphinde usize ukusebenzisa izinjongo nokuhlinzekwa kwePandemic Accord okwamanje okusaxoxwa ngayo yiWHO Member State. Kulandela lokho okufakiwe nezingxoxo emihlanganweni yababambe iqhaza, iWHO imema ababambisani kulesimemezelo somhlaba sokwenza ukuwandisa ukuzilungiselela kobhubhane legciwane lokuphefumula. translations.reference: WHO will continue to develop and disseminate guidance on specific diseases as needed. The launch of the initiative is taking place on the last day of meetings with over 120 individuals representing Member States, partners, and the WHO secretariat, to define the path forward for PRET, including the development of a global implementation roadmap for respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. The PRET Initiative’s first module focuses on respiratory pathogens, including influenza, coronaviruses, and respiratory syncytial virus. Given the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and the possible threat of avian influenza, this module will enable countries to critically review, test, and update their respiratory pandemic planning efforts to ensure they have the functional capacities and capabilities in place. A process is underway to identify the next group of pathogens, such as arboviruses, to be addressed under this initiative. This will follow priorities identified through the ten proposals to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience (HEPR). The PRET Initiative ushers in a new era for pandemic preparedness and represents an evolution of WHO’s core activities to support all Member States in strengthening health emergency preparedness, prevention, and response capacities and capabilities. The initiative can also serve to operationalize the objectives and provisions of the Pandemic Accord, which is currently being negotiated by WHO Member States. Following inputs and discussions among meeting participants, WHO invites stakeholders to this global call to action to advance respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. Call to Action

translations.source: AmaMember State eWHO, nababambisani basebenza ndawonye ukuhlonza nokuhambisa phambili ukwenza izenzo zokuqinisa izwe, isifunda, nokuzilungiselela komhlaba kwezobhubhane nokuvela kwezifo ezithelelanayo ezisabisayo. Siyayibona indima yomphakathi nayo yonke imikhakha sidinga ukulinganisa nokugcina umfutho ekupheleni kwesakile yokusaba nokunganaki lokho kujwayelekile ekuzilungiseleni kobhubhane nokuphendula. Sikhumbula izifundo zobhubhane olwedlule ne-ephidemikhi, ikakhulukazi lezo ezidalwa amagciwane okuphefumula. Lawo magciwane azoqhubeka nokuletha ukusabisa komhlaba okukhulu ngamandla okudala izinkinga ezinkulu nokufa, izinhlelo zempilo ezingaphezu kwamandla, ukwehlisa ezomnotho zomhlaba, ukwengeza ukungalingani, okukhona ekungeneni kumathuluzi omabili okuvikela ubhubhane nokunakekelwa ezempilo kubona bonke abantu. Lesi yisimemezelo sokwenza ukuqhubeza ukuzilungiselela kobhubhane nokuvela kokwesaba emhlabeni. Ukusebenza kokuzilungiselela kuncike kubukhulu bokuhlela nokwenza kokuxhumana. Ukuzimisela kweqoqo kuzenze ezilandelayo kuzobonisa ukuqhubeka okutholakele ngoZibandlela 2025 futhi kuzochazwa (Ukuzilungiselela nokubekezela kokusabisayo okuvelayo (PRET) ukubheka umhlahlandlela: Ukulungiselelwa kokubuyekeziwe ukuhlela okuqinisekisa izenzo zokuhamba phambili nokucabanga izifundo zezigemegeme ezedlule. Ukubona ingcuphe efakwe amagciwane ezokuphefumula, ukuhlela kobhubhane lamagciwane ezokuphefumula ancike kuzingqikithi ezihlonzwe kusifundo sePRET #1: Ukuhlelela ubhubhane lwamagciwane ezokuphefumula kuhamba phambili
translations.reference: WHO, Member States, and partners are working together to identify and implement priority actions to strengthen national, regional, and global preparedness for pandemics and emerging infectious disease threats. We recognize the role of communities and all sectors, and the need for harmonized and sustained momentum to end the cycle of panic and neglect that is all too common in pandemic preparedness and response. We recall lessons from previous epidemics and pandemics, especially those caused by respiratory pathogens. These pathogens have and will continue to pose a significant global threat with the potential to cause tremendous morbidity and mortality, overwhelm health systems, de-stabilize the global economy, and exacerbate inequities, which exist in the access to both the tools to prevent pandemics and health care for all people. This is a Call to Action to accelerate preparedness for pandemics and emerging threats globally. Effective preparedness relies on robust planning and coordinated action. A collective commitment on the following actions will see progress achieved by December 2025 as will be described in the (Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET) monitoring framework: Update preparedness plans that affirm priority actions and that have considered learnings from past events. Recognizing the risk posed by respiratory pathogens, planning for a respiratory pathogen pandemic based on the themes identified in the PRET Module #1: Planning for Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics is a priority

translations.source: Ukunyusa ukuxhumana kubabambisani ekuzilungiseleni kobhubhane ukuhlela ngezinhlelo zokuxhumana nokusebenzisana. Phakathi nalomsebenzi ukuqinisekisa ukulingana ukulungela obhubhane olulandelayo ndawonye. Ukwenza komphakathi wonke kuyadingeka ukwenza inqubekela phambili evezwe kule simemezelo sokwenza. translations.reference: Increase connectivity among stakeholders in pandemic preparedness planning through systematic coordination and cooperation. This includes building equitable systems; conducting joint exercises; and sharing information on good practices, challenges, and opportunities. Dedicate sustained investments, financing and monitoring of pandemic preparedness with a particular focus on addressing the gaps identified during past pandemics and epidemics. The COVID-19 pandemic response has demonstrated what can be achieved with political commitment, community engagement and funding. At the heart of this work is to ensure equity to be ready for the next pandemic together. Whole-of-society action is needed to make the progress outlined in this Call to Action. Implementation should therefore strengthen the resilience of communities; maintain, sustain, and build on routine systems; and leverage broader capacities for emergency preparedness and response. This Call to Action was released at the Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET): Global Meeting for Future Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics held on 24-26 April 2023 in Geneva, Switzerland.

translations.source: I-WHO inxenxa ukunyuswa kokwenza ngamathuluzi aphakanyisiwe ukulwa nomalaleveva Ukumaka uSuku lukaMalaleveva eMhlabeni, Inhlangano Yezempilo Yomhlaba (WHO) izokhipha isimememzelo sokunyusa ukwenza kokungenelela okukhona nokusha ukusindisa izimpilo kumalaleveva. Okungafika kuzigidi ezingu-1.5 izingane ezisengcupheni enkulu yokugula nokufa okusuka kumalaleveva eGhana, eKenya naseMalawi manje sebethole umgomo wokuqala kamalaleveva, RTS, S/AS01 (RTS,S), siyabonga kuhlelo lokuhlola oluqhubekayo oluxhunyaniswa yiWHO. Ukuhlolwa komgomo wamalaleveva, owethulwe ngo-2019,kwenyusa ukulingana kokuthola ukuvikelwa kumalaleveva kulabo ababuthaka kakhulu futhi kusindisa izimpilo. Ukwenziwe kakhulu, iWHO ilinganisela ukuthi umgomo kamalaleveva ungasingisa izimpilo eziyizinkulungwane eziyishumi zezingane ngonyaka. "Sinamathuluzi ukwehlisa umalaleveva,iphekheji yokungenenela ebandakanya ukulawula ivektha,imithi yokuvikela, ukuhlola nokwelapha," kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO. "Lokhu kulandelwa ngumgomo ophephile nosebenzayo kamalaleveva, ongasindisa izimpilo eziyizinkulungwane eziyishumi zezingane unyaka ngamunye. Ngotshalomali olugcinekile nokunyuka kwemizamo ukufika kulabo abasengcupheni kakhulu, ukuqedwa kwamalaleveva emazweni amaningi sekuyafika. "Usuku lukaMalaleveva eMhlabeni 2023 lumakwe ngaphansi kwengqikithi, "Isikhathi sokuwqeda umalaleveva, tshala, yenza kabusha yenza". Kule ngqikithi, iWHO inxenxa okunye ukwenza okusebenzayo kwamathuluzi akhona namasu okuvikela, ukuxilonga nokwelapha umalaleveva, ikakhulukazi kulesosibalo esingcindezelekile.
translations.reference: WHO urges increased implementation of recommended tools to combat malaria Marking World Malaria Day, the World Health Organization (WHO) is issuing a call for increased implementation of new and existing interventions to save lives from malaria. Nearly 1.5 million children at high risk of illness and death from malaria in Ghana, Kenya and Malawi have now received their first dose of the first malaria vaccine, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), thanks to an ongoing pilot programme coordinated by WHO. The malaria vaccine pilots, launched in 2019, are increasing equity in access to malaria prevention for the most vulnerable and are saving lives. If implemented broadly, WHO estimates that malaria vaccines could save the lives of tens of thousands of children each year. “We have the tools to drive down malaria, a package of interventions that includes vector control, preventive medicines, testing, and treatment,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. “These are joined by a safe and effective malaria vaccine, which could save the lives of tens of thousands children every year. With sustained investment and scaled-up efforts to reach those most at risk, malaria elimination in many countries is in reach. "World Malaria Day 2023 is being marked under the theme “Time to deliver zero malaria: invest, innovate, implement”. Within this theme, WHO is urging more effective implementation of available tools and strategies to prevent, diagnose and treat malaria, particularly among marginalized populations.

Significant progress has been made in reaching the most vulnerable populations, and the elimination of malaria in many countries is within sight.” The 2023 World Malaria Day is marked by the theme, “Eliminate Malaria, Prevent Misconceptions, and Do It Again, Do It Again.”  In this theme, the WHO is exploring other effective tools for existing and protective measures, as well as testing and treating malaria, particularly in high-burden settings.

translations.source: Ngokombiko wokugcina wamalaleveva emhlabeni,oshicilelwe ngoZibandlela 2022, bekunesilinganiso sika-247 wezigidi zamacala amasha kamalaleveva ngo-2021. I-WHO African Region iyaqhubeka ukuthwala umthwalo onzima wesifo- okuballelwa esilinganiswenisa ku-95% wamacala kamalaleveva (u-234 wezigidi) no-96% wokufa (593 000) ngo-2021. Kungafika ku-80% ukufa okudalwa umalaleveva eSifundeni se-Afrika obukukubantwana abangaphansi kweminyaka emihlanu. Amasu amasha namathuluzi Amazweasenze inqubekela phambili ekwandiseni ukungena kumisebenzi yamalaleveva kusibalo sabantu esisengcupheni kakhulu. Ngaphandle kokuqhubeka abantu abaningi abasengcupheni yamalaleveva basantula ukungena emisebenzini engavimba, noma itthole bese yelapha isifo. Izingqinamba ekwandeni kokungena kumisebenzi kamalaleveva sezihlanganisisiwe,ikakhulukazi esu-Saharan Afrika, ngobhubhane lweCOVID-19, ukujika izinkinga zobuntu, uxhasomali olunezivimbo, izinhlelo zokubheka ezintekenteke, ukwehla ekwenzeni kwamathuluzi okulwa ngqo nomalaleveva. Ukubhekana nalokhu okusabisayo nokweseka amazwe ekwakheni izinhlelo zokubekezela zamalaleveva, iWHO isanda kushicilela amasu amasha nomhlahlandlela: isu elisha lelinesidakamizwa esingenamalaleveva ukuvika eAfrika; umzamo omusha wokumisa ukusabalala kweAnophelesi stephensi ezindaweni zasemadolobheni;
translations.reference: According to the latest World malaria report, published in December 2022, there were an estimated 247 million new cases of malaria in 2021. The WHO African Region continues to shoulder the heaviest burden of the disease – accounting for an estimated 95% of all malaria cases (234 million) and 96% of all deaths (593 000) in 2021. Nearly 80% of malaria deaths in the African Region were among children under the age of five. New strategies and tools Countries have made some progress in expanding access to malaria services for most-at-risk populations. Despite some progress, many people at high risk of malaria still lack access to services that can prevent, detect and treat the disease. Challenges in expanding access to malaria services have been compounded, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, by the COVID-19 pandemic, converging humanitarian crises, restricted funding, weak surveillance systems, and declines in the effectiveness of core malaria-fighting tools. To address these threats and support countries in building more resilient malaria programmes, WHO recently published new strategies and frameworks, including: a new strategy to contain antimalarial drug resistance in Africa; a new initiative to stop the spread of Anopheles stephensi in urban environments;

“As of the latest data released in December 2022, there were 247 million new cases of malaria in 2021. The WHO African Region continues to bear a heavy burden of the disease, accounting for 95% of new malaria cases (234 million) and 96% of malaria deaths (593,000) in 2021.  This represents up to 80% of deaths caused by malaria in the African region among children under five years of age. New tools and strategies have advanced progress in increasing access to malaria interventions for a large proportion of the population.  However, many vulnerable people continue to be unable to access prevention or treatment services for the disease.  Challenges to increasing access to malaria interventions are compounded, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, due to the impact of COVID-19, urbanization challenges, conflict and instability, inadequate funding, and insufficient tools for direct malaria control.  To address this uncertainty and strengthen countries’ malaria preparedness plans, the WHO has recently published new tools and guidance: a new drug-free intervention for malaria in Africa; a new initiative to control the spread of *Anopheles stephensi* in urban areas;”

translations.reference: a new framework, developed jointly by WHO and UN-Habitat, to guide city leaders in urban malaria control. a new toolkit to help countries assess their malaria surveillance systems and identify areas for investment WHO has also increased the transparency, flexibility and access to its malaria recommendations. The consolidated WHO Guidelines for malaria are now available through two digital platforms: MAGICapp and the “Malaria Toolkit” app. WHO encourages countries to tailor the recommendations to local disease settings for maximum impact. Prospects for new interventions Continued investment in the development and deployment of new malaria vaccines and next-generation tools will be key to achieving the 2030 global malaria targets. A second malaria vaccine, the R21/Matrix-M (R21) vaccine, if approved, could help close the sizable gap between supply and demand and further reduce child illness and death from malaria. It is a priority for WHO to continue the thorough and efficient expert review of the R21 malaria vaccine once additional key safety and efficacy data from the ongoing R21 phase 3 trial are available and provided to WHO. Phased introductions of the RTS,S malaria vaccine in additional countries in Africa are expected to begin in early 2024.

translations.source: Uhlelo loKudlulisela ezobuchwepheshe i-mRNA iya esigabeni esilandelayo ekuthuthukeni kwayo Ukwethulwa kwehabhu yezobuchwepheshe imRNA indawo e-Afrigen, eNingizimu Afrika. Ababambisani bahlangane eKapa ukuxoxa ngesigaba esilandelayo semRNA umgomo ngokuthuthukisa nokwabelana ngolwazi. Ngaphezu kwababambiqhaza abangu-200 bamazwe angaphandle abasebenza ngoHlelo loKudlulisela lezoBuchwepheshe beMRNA abahlangane eKapa kulelisonto ukuze bahlangane bebonana. Ukujoyinwa iNhlangano yeZempilo eMhlabeni (WHO) uMqondisi Jikelele uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus noDkt Joe Phaahla uNgqongqoshe weZempilo noNgqongqoshe Wezimboni nokuhweba uMnumzane Ebrahim waseNingizimu Afrika ndawonye nezikhulu ezisezingeni eliphezulu eziphuma emazweni axhasile, lomhlangano owehlukile ubheke ukuqhubeka kusukela ukwethula kweWHO neMedicines Patent Pool (MPP) ngoNhlangulana 2021. "Ngiyajabula ukuba lapha eKapa kanye nababambisani bethu ukuzokweseka imodeli egcinekayo yokudluliselwa kobuchwepheshe mRNA ukunikeza amazwe anemali engenayo encane kuya kwephakathi nendawo ukuthola ngokulingana umgomo kanye neminye imikhiqizo yezempilo esindisa izimpilo zabantu," kusho uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO, uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "Ngijabule kakhulu ngokuzuziwe kubona bonke ababambe iqhaza kulephrojekthi,ngaphansi kweminyaka emibili sesikhombisile ukuthi uma sisebenza ngokubambisana, siphumelela sonke." Ababambiqhaza bomhlangano kufaka ababambisani bomkhiqizo wasemazweni angu-15 oHlelweni, ongoti abaphambili, imboni abameli bomphakathi kanye nabaxhasimali. Ngesikhathi somhlangano wezinsuku esinhlanu, ababambiqhaza bazokwabelana ngokuqhubeka baxoxe ngokubucayi kwezinto zokugcineka koHlelo njengezinkinga zegunyabunikazi nezindlela zokulawula kanyenesayensi yezobuchwepheshe be-mRNA nokusebenza okubalulekile okuhlobene neLMIC kwezinye izndawo zezifo njengeHIV nesifo sofuba. Ubhubhane lweCOVID-19 sekukale ngaphansi ukuthi ukungalingani okuphelele kukhona ekutholeni imikhiqizo yezempilo, ikakhulukazi imigomo.
translations.reference: mRNA Technology Transfer Programme moves to the next phase of its development Inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen, South Africa. Partners gather in Cape Town to discuss the next phase of the mRNA vaccine development and knowledge sharing. Over 200 international participants working with the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme met in Cape Town this week for their first face-to-face meeting. Joined by World Health Organization (WHO) Director General, Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus and Dr. Joe Phaahla, Minister of Health and Minister of Trade and Industry, Mr. Ebrahim Patel of South Africa together with high-level officials from funding countries, this unique meeting reviewed the progress since WHO and Medicines Patent Pool (MPP) launched it in June 2021. “I am delighted to be here in Cape Town with our partners to support a sustainable model for mRNA technology transfer to give low- and middle-income countries equitable access to vaccines and other lifesaving health products,” said WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “I am immensely proud of the achievement of all those involved in this project; in less than two years we have shown that when we work collaboratively, we succeed collectively.” The meeting participants include biomanufacturing partners from 15 countries in the Programme, leading experts, industry, civil society representatives, and funders. During the five-day meeting, participants will share progress and discuss critical enablers for the sustainability of the Programme such as intellectual property issues and regulatory aspects, as well as the science of mRNA technologies and key applications relevant to LMICs in other disease areas such as HIV and tuberculosis. The COVID-19 pandemic has underscored that gross inequity exists in access to health products, especially vaccines.

The mRNA technology transfer program is moving to the next phase in its development. The launch of the mRNA technology hub is taking place at Afrigen, in South Africa. More than 200 international participants, including those working on the mRNA Technology Transfer Program, will gather in Cape Town this week to meet and exchange ideas. translations.source: Kusukela ngoNdasa 2023, ngaphezu kweminyaka emithathu ngemva kokuthi Iwho imemezele iCOVID-19 njengePublic Health of International Concern (PHEIC), u-69.7% wesibalo sabantu emhlabeni sebethole okungenani idosi eyodwa yomgomo weCOVID-19. Okuqaphelekayo lengxenye izohlala ingaphansi kuka-30% emazweni anemali engenayo encane (amaLIC). Loli Hlelo luhlose ukuxhasa ekutholeno okulinganayo emigomweni ye-mRNA ngokunyusa ukuhambisa kwamandla okukhiqiza okugcinekayo kuwona wonke amaLMIC, ukwandisa okwesifunda nokubambisana kwaphakathi nezifunda nokudevelopha nokusiza ukusebenza kwasendaweni ngokwenziwa kokuqeqeshwa nokwesekwa ngongoti. Yikuphi okwehlukile kumodeli yoKudluliselwa kweZobuchwepheshe kwe-mRNA kuhlelo olunezinto eziningi oluvumela ukwabelana ngobuchwepheshe,ukuthola okuningi ukuze ukukhiqizwa ngokwesifunda nendawo kulabo abadingayo kungafinyelela ngokuphindiwe. UDkt Phaahla, uNgqongqoshe weZempilo eNingizimu Afrika, uthe: "Esikubona namhlanje lapha,umzumzu womlando, uHlelo oluhlose ukusiza iLMIC ngohlelokuxhumana lokubambisana emhlabeni. Ngiyajabula ukubona inqubekela phambili eyenziwe esikhathini esifishane kangaka, siyakwamukela ukwesekwa okusuka emazweni amaningi ahlukene-amazwe afana neNingizimu Afrika anokukhiqizwa okuqinile anamandla, futhi sizimisele ukusebenza ndawonye, ukufunda nokwabelana kuthina sonke." Ungqongqoshe Blade Nzimande weMfundo ePhakeme neSayensi, uthe: "Umbono wethu weHabhu yoKudlulisela ubuchwepheshe be-mRNA kungaphezu kweCOVID-19 nje. Amandla okuthi siyakha afuna usizo kuthina ukubhekana nobhubhane lwesikhathi esizayo imigomo yalo engasebenzisa inkundla efanayo yobuchwepheshe be-mRNA. Kungumbono osubeke amehlo awo ekubhekaneni nezifo ezivamile ezindaweni zethu njengoMalaleveva, isifo sofuba, iHIV ne-AIDS." Lomcimbi uphinde umake uhambo olubalulekile loHlelo nokwethulwa kwendawo yeHabhu yobuchwepheshe ne-mRNA e-Afrigen eKapa kukhona uDkt Tedros noDkt Matshidiso Moeti weWHO Regional Director for Afrika, Charles Gore uMqondisi oMkhulu weMPP kanye noNgqongqoshe abahlonishwayo ababamba iqhaza ekusikweni kweribhini emcimbini e-Afrigen.
translations.reference: As of March 2023, more than three years after WHO declared COVID-19 a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), 69.7% of the global population had received at least one dose of a COVID-19 vaccine. Notably, this proportion still remains below 30% in low-income countries (LICs) . This Programme aims to contribute to equitable access to mRNA vaccines by increasing the distribution of sustainable manufacturing capacity across LMICs, enhancing regional and inter-regional collaborations, and developing and empowering a local workforce through tailored and inclusive training and expert support. What is unique in the mRNA Technology Transfer model is the multilateral process that allows sharing of technologies to multiple recipients so that through local and regional production those in need can be reached rapidly. Dr Phaahla, Minister of Health South Africa, said: “What we see here today, is a moment in history, a Programme that is aimed at empowering LMICs through a global collaborative network. I am thrilled to see the progress made in such a relatively short time and welcome the support from so many different countries - countries like South Africa that have a strong vibrant biomanufacturing capacity and that are willing to work together, learn from and share with each other.” Minister Blade Nzimande of Higher Education and Science, said: “Ours is the vision of the mRNA Technology Transfer Hub beyond just COVID-19. The capabilities we are building are looking to empower us to deal with other future pandemics whose vaccines could use the same mRNA technology platform. It is a vision that has set its eyes on diseases that are prevalent in our environments such as Malaria, Tuberculosis and HIV and AIDS.” This occasion also marks an important milestone for the Programme with the inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen in Cape Town in the presence of Dr. Tedros and Dr. Matshidiso Moeti WHO Regional Director for Africa, Charles Gore Executive Director of MPP and the Honorable Ministers who took part in a ribbon cutting ceremony at Afrigen.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Zulu document into English.gs:

Since November 2023, more than three years after COVID-19 was declared a Public Health of International Concern (PHEIC), 69.7% of the global population has received at least one dose of a COVID-19 vaccine. Notably, this percentage is often below 30% in low-income countries (LICs). This Initiative aims to support equitable access to mRNA vaccines by scaling up vaccine manufacturing capacity securely in all LMICs, expanding regional production, and fostering collaboration between regional and developed countries, and assisting local implementation through training and support programs. It offers a unique approach to mRNA technology transfer, enabling the sharing of expertise and facilitating increased local production for those in need, all at an affordable cost. 

Dr. Phaahla, the Minister of Health for South Africa, stated: “What we see today, the legacy of the past, this Initiative aims to assist LMICs through a global partnership mechanism. I am pleased to see the progress made in recent times, we welcome support from many different countries – countries like South Africa with proven manufacturing capacity, and we remain committed to working together, learning and sharing with each other.”

Minister Blade Nzimande of Higher Education and Science stated: “Our vision for the mRNA Technology Hub goes beyond COVID-19. The capacity to build allows us to seek assistance in addressing future health challenges using the same platform of mRNA technology. It’s a vision that looks ahead to common diseases in our regions such as malaria, tuberculosis, HIV and AIDS.”

The event also marked an important milestone for this Initiative and the launch of the mRNA Technology Hub in Africa at Afrigen in Cape Town, with Dr. Tedros and Dr. Matshidiso Moeti, WHO Regional Director for Africa, Charles Gore, Senior Manager of MPP, and esteemed honored guests participating in the event at Afrigen.

translations.source: USolwazi Petro Terblance, uMqondisi oMkhulu we-Afrigen, uthe: "Iqembu lonke le-Afrigen liyajabula ukufika kulokhu okubalulekile nokuqedwa kwenkundla yezobuchwepheshe be-mRNA. Lenkundla inendawo ekupheleni kokuthuthukiswa komgomo mRNA nokukhiqizwa kwendawo lapho ihabhu ye-mRNA yomgomo weCOVID-19 ikhandidethi AfriVac 2121 njengamanje isezingeni lesikalo esiphezulu. Ngaphezu kwezinyanga ezingu-18 Afrigen isihambe uguquko olukhulu ngokwesekwa kohlelokuxhumana lababambisani nabeluleki abenza loluHlelo. Sesikhulise amandla ethu nokukwazi ukuhlangabezana nezinga elihle eliphezulu lokuthuthukiswa komgomo we-mRNA, ukusiza injongo yokwakha ukukwazi okugcinekile kumaLMIC ukukhiqiza imigomo ye-mRNA.". I-Afrigen isisungulwe ngempumelelo uhlelo lokukhiqizwa kwemigomo yeCOVID-19 ngesikalo selebhu njengamanje kuyanyuka uhlelo ngezinga elifanele lokukhiqiza ngezigaba imigomo ukuthi isetshenziswe ngesigaba sesibili noma sokuqala sokuhlola kwezokwelapha kumazinga eGMP. Kuhlelo oluhambisanayo, i-Afrigen izoqhubeka nokwenza ukuqeqesha nokudlulisela ezobuchwepheshe kubabambisani bohlelokuxhumana. Abaxhasi badlala indima ebalulekile ekwesekeni uHlelo ngesamba soxhaso kuze kube yimanje ngezigidi ezingu-117 neFrance ekubeni ngeyokuqala ukuxhasa umsebenzi wokudlulisela ezobuchwepheshe be-mRNA. Lokhu kuletha iminikelo eyisamba yeCanada kuHlelo lwezigidi ezingu-45 kumadola aseCanada.

We have grown our capability and capacity to meet the highest quality standards of mRNA vaccine development, serving the objective to build sustainable capacity in LMICs to produce mRNA vaccines”. This brings Canada’s total contribution to the Programme up to 45 million Canadian dollars.

translations.source: Ukuqokwa kobuholi oubalulekile okwenziwelwe ukuhambisa indlela yesu nemizamo yeWHO Inhlangano yeZempilo yoMhlaba (WHO) sebeqoke izikhulu eziphezulu iminyaka emihlanu ukuba ngabaholi eqembini labaholi ekomkhulu eGeneva. Ukuqokwa okusha kulandela ukuphinde kuqokwe uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus kuhlandla lesibili leminyaka emihlanu njengoMqondisi Jikelele. Ubuholi bonkebeqembu buhlanganiswe ukuhambisana nokuhamba phambili kweNhlangano kuleminyaka emihlanu ezayo nokusebenzisana noMqondisi Jikelele ukuhambisa okuya kukhona okuhamba phambili ne-ajenda yentshisekelo yoguquko lweWHO. Ukuqhuba inqubekela phambili yokwenza kweWHO 13th General Programme of WORK (GPW13) ukuthola okuqondiwe "okungezigidigidi ezintathu" nokuhlobene nezempilo zeSuatainable Development Goals, umsebenzi weWHO kuleminyaka emihlanu ezayo egxile kokuhamba phambili okuhlanu: ukwenza ukulandelana ngendlela okuya ekugqugquzeleni ezempilo nokuvikela isifo ngokubhekana nomsuka ozidalayo; ushintsho olwenzekayo kuzinhlelo zezempilo okuya ekunakekelweni kwezempilo zokuqala; ukuqinisa izinhlelo zokuzilungiselela okuphuthumayo nokuphendula; amahhanisi amandla esayensi,ukwenza kabusha,idatha nezobuchwepheshe ukwenzangcono ezempilo; kanye nokuqinisa iWHO njengabanegunya lokuhola kwezempilo zomhlaba nokuthola umthelela emazweni.
translations.reference: Key leadership appointments made to drive WHO strategic direction and initiatives The World Health Organization (WHO) has appointed five new senior figures to its headquarters leadership team in Geneva. The new appointments follow the reappointment of Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus to his second five-year term as Director-General. The overall leadership team has been consolidated to align with the Organization’s priorities for the next five years and will work closely with the Director-General to drive forward these priorities and WHO’s ambitious transformation agenda. To accelerate progress on implementation of WHO’s 13th General Programme of Work (GPW13), and achievement of its “triple billion” targets and the health-related Sustainable Development Goals, WHO’s work for the coming five years is focused on five priorities: making a paradigm shift towards promoting health and preventing disease by addressing its root causes; radically reorienting health systems towards primary health care; strengthening the systems for health emergency preparedness and response; harnessing the power of science, innovation, data and technologies to advance health; and strengthening WHO as the leading authority on global health and to achieve impact in countries.

“The election of leaders is crucial to ensure the effective representation of WHO’s global health initiatives. The World Health Organization (WHO) has elected six high-level officials for five-year terms as leaders of the executive board at the World Health Assembly in Geneva. The new appointments follow the re-election of Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus as Director-General for a second five-year term. All the leadership bodies have been consolidated to support WHO’s progress over the next five years and to work with the Director-General to advance the organization’s strategic agenda for change.  To drive forward the WHO’s 13th General Programme of Work (GPW13) and achieve the target of “three billion” and related sustainable development goals for health, WHO’s work over the next five years will focus on accelerating progress in health and disease prevention; transforming health systems towards primary healthcare; strengthening emergency preparedness and response systems; harnessing the power of science, innovation, data, and expertise to improve health; and strengthening WHO as a global health leader and finding a path forward for countries.”

translations.source: Qembu labaholi ekomkhulu, linenamba elinganayo kubantu besifazane nabesilisa, bazosebenza ngokuhlanganyela nesifunda seWHO namahhovisi emazweni. Amalungu amasha eqembu lobuholi ekomkhulu:uDkt Jeremy Farrar ozoba nguSosayensi omkhulu weWHO kusukela ngomhlaka-8 kuNhlaba 2023. Ukuqokwa kukaDkt Farrar kwamenyezelwa ngoZibandlela 2022. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Farrar uzobheka umkhakha wezesayensi, ukuhlanganisa ndawonye ngoti abaphambili nohlelokuxhumana kwezesayensi nokwenza kabusha emhlabeni wonke ukuyalela, ukudevelopha nokuhambisa ezempilo ezisezingeni eliphezulu inqubomgomo nemisebenzi kubantu abayidinga kakhulu. Ngaphambi kokujoyina iWHO uDkt Farrar ubenguMqondisi weWellcome Trust. Ngaphambi kokujoyina Wellcome ngo-2013 uDkt Farrar uchithe iminyaka engu-17 njengoMqondisi weClinical Reaserch Unit esiBhadlela seTropical Diseases eVietnam lapho ukucwaninga kwakhe kwakugxile kwezempilo emhlabeni ngokugxila ekuveleni kwezifo ezithelelanayo. UDkt Farrar ungumcwaningi wezokwelapha neziqu zobudokotela kanye nePHD. UDkt Ailan Li uzoba nguMsizi Mqondisi Jikelele weUniversal Health Coverage,Healthier Populations kusukela ngomhlaka-8 kuNhlaba 2023. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Li izobheka imizamo yeNhlangano ukugqugquzela ukubangcono kwezempilo nokuphila ngokungenelela okuhlobene nezendawo ezenhlalakahle, ezempilo zokubhekene nezomnotho okufaka ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu, ukulawula ugwayi, ukuphepha kwamakhemikhali, ukuphepha komgwaqo, izinhlelo zokudla nokunomsoco, ukusebenza siqu, ukungcoliswa komoya nokwemisebe yelana ngendlela ye-One Health. UDkt Li ubelokhu esebenza njengomele iWHO eKingdom of Cambodia kusukela ngomhlaka-15 kuNtulikazi 2019.
translations.reference: The headquarters leadership team, comprising equal numbers of women and men, will work jointly with WHO regional and country offices. New members of the headquarters leadership team: Dr Jeremy Farrar will become WHO’s Chief Scientist as of 8 May 2023. The appointment of Dr Farrar was previously announced in December 2022. In this role Dr Farrar will oversee the Science Division, bringing together the best experts and networks in science and innovation from around the world to guide, develop and deliver high quality health policies and services to the people who need them most. Prior to joining WHO Dr Farrar was Director of the Wellcome Trust. Before joining Wellcome in 2013 Dr Farrar spent 17 years as Director of the Clinical Research Unit at the Hospital for Tropical Diseases in Vietnam where his research interests were in global health with a focus on emerging infectious diseases. Dr Farrar is a clinician researcher with a medical degree and a PhD. Dr Ailan Li will become Assistant Director-General for Universal Health Coverage, Healthier Populations as of 8 May 2023. In this role Dr Li will oversee the Organization’s efforts to promote better health and well-being through interventions relating to the environmental, social, and economic determinants of health, including climate change, tobacco control, chemical safety, road safety, food systems and nutrition, physical activity, air pollution and radiation, through a One Health approach. Dr Li has been serving as the WHO Representative to the Kingdom of Cambodia since 15 July 2019.

translations.reference: Prior to this role, Dr Li served as Regional Emergency Director for the WHO Health Emergencies Programme in the WHO Regional Office for the Western Pacific. Dr Nakatani has been serving as the Director of the Cancer and Disease Control Division of the Japanese Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare and as the Technical Advisor to the Minister of State for the COVID-19 Vaccination Task Force in Japan. Dr Nakatani holds a medical degree and a PhD in public health. Dr Razia Pendse will become the Chef de Cabinet as of 4 May 2023. In this role Dr Pendse will head the Director-General’s Office, helping to drive the Organization’s priorities and initiatives, and will ensure alignment within the WHO leadership team and across the three levels of WHO. Dr Pendse has been serving as the Director of Healthier Populations and Noncommunicable Diseases in the WHO Regional Office for South-East Asia and was previously the WHO Representative to Sri Lanka. Dr Pendse holds a medical degree and master's degree in public health.

translations.source: UDkt Jerome Salomon uzoba Ngumsizi waMqondisi Jikelele wezokukhavwa kweZempilo eMhlabeni, izifo okuxhunyanwa ngazo nokungaxhunyanwa ngazo kusukela ngomhlaka-17 Mbasa 2023. Kulesisikhundla uDkt Salamon uzobheka iphothifoliyo enkulu yezinhlelo zezobuchwepheshe ezibheka iHIV, igciwane lehephathithisi, ukuthelelana kwezifo zocansi, isifo sofuba, umalalaeveva, izifo ezidumile ezinganakiwe, ezempilo zengqondo, isifo sokusebenzisa izidakamizwa izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo njengezifo zenhliziyo, ushukena, izifo zondendende zokuphefumua kanye nomdlavuza. UDkt Salomon ubesebenza njengoMqondisi Jikelele weZempilo kubungqongqoshe beZempilo nokuvikela eFrance. Wayeyilungu leBhodi labaphathi beWHO ngaphambi kokuqokwa kwakhe futhi nesipiliyoni sakhe esikhulu ekuphathweni kwezinhlelo zempilo,izifo okuxhunyanwa ngazo nezempilo yomphakathi kumazwe angaphandle. UDkt Salomon uneziqu zobudokotela,izqu zemastazi kwezempilo yomphakathi kanye nePHD ku-ephidemiloji. Lamaphothifolo amalungu akhona eqembu lobuholi ekomkhulu: uDkt Samira Asma uzoqhubeka njengoMsizi Mqondisi Jikelele weMininingo, ukuhlaziya nokulethwa kwemithelela. UDkt Asma ubheke iphothifoliyo eqinisekisa ukuthi idatha yezempilo ikhona futhi iyatholakala, futhi isetshenziswa ukwenzangcono imiphumela yezempilo emhlabeni wonke, okufaka ukulandelela nokuqhuba ukuqhubeka okubheke kokuqondiwe okungamabhiliyoni amathathu futhi okuhlobene nesempilo yeSustainable Development Goals. Ngaphambi kokujoyina iWHO ngo-2018, uDkt Asma ubambe izikhundla zobuholi e-U.S. Centers for Disease Contrel and Prevention (CDC) iminyaka engaphezu kwamashumi amabili. Uneziqu zobudokotela kwezokuhlinza amazinyo kanye neziqu kwezempilo yomphakathi. UDkt Bruce Alyward useqokelwe ukuba nguMsizi aMqondisi Jikelele weze-Universal Health Coverage, Life Course Division kusuka ngomhlaka-4 kuNhlaba 2023.
translations.reference: Dr Jérôme Salomon will become Assistant Director-General for Universal Health Coverage, Communicable and Noncommunicable Diseases as of 17 April 2023. In this role Dr Salomon will oversee a broad portfolio of technical programmes covering HIV, viral hepatitis, sexually-transmitted infections, tuberculosis, malaria, neglected tropical diseases, mental health, substance use disorders, and noncommunicable diseases such as cardiovascular disease, diabetes, chronic respiratory diseases and cancer. Dr Salomon has been serving as the Director-General for Health at the Ministry of Health and Prevention of France. He was a Member of the WHO Executive Board prior to his appointment and has extensive experience in health systems management, communicable diseases and international public health. Dr Salomon holds a medical degree, a master’s degree in public health and a PhD in epidemiology. The portfolios of existing members of the headquarters leadership team: Dr Samira Asma will continue as the Assistant Director-General for Data, Analytics and Delivery for Impact. Dr Asma oversees a portfolio which ensures that health data are reliable and accessible, and are used to improve health outcomes worldwide, including tracking and accelerating progress towards the triple billion targets and the health-related Sustainable Development Goals. Prior to joining WHO in 2018, Dr Asma held leadership positions at the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) for over two decades. She holds a doctorate degree in dental surgery and a master's degree in public health. Dr Bruce Aylward has been appointed Assistant Director-General of the Universal Health Coverage, Life Course Division as of 4 May 2023.

UDkt Boehme uneziqu zobudokotela nediploma ekuphatheni nezempilo yomphakathi. UDKt chikwe Ihekweazu uzoqhubeka nokuba nguMszi waMqondisi Jikelele weDivision of Health Emergency Interlligence and Survillance Systems kuHlelo lwezokuohuthumayo. Njengengxenye yephothifoliyo yakhe, uDkt Ihekweazu uhola iHabhu yeWHO yePandemic and Epidemic Intelligence, ukuhlanganisa ndawonye ababambisani ukubhekana nobhubhane lwekusasa nezingcuphe ze-ephidemikhi ngokungena kangcono kumininingo amandla okuhlaziya namathuluzi okuthatha izinqumo. Ngaphambi kokujoyina iWHO uDkT Ihekweazu waba nguMqondisi Jikelele wokuqala weNigeria Centre fo Disease Control NCDC) kusuka ngo-2016 kuya ku-2021. UDkt Ihekweazu uneziqu zobudokotela ngokugxila ku-ephidomoloji yezifo ezithelelanayo kanye neziqu zemastazi kwezempilo yomphakathi. UDkt Michael Ryan uzoqhubeka nokuba nguMqondisi omkhulu woHlelo lweZiphuthumayo zeMpilo kuWHO. UDkt Ryan ubephethe ezokuphuthumayo zezempilo kuWHO eminyakeni engu-25 eyedlule futhi usanda kuhola ukuphendula kweziphuthumayo kweWHO ngokobhubhane lweCOVID-19. Uhola ukuphendula kweWHO ekubhedukeni kwezifo izinkinga zabantu kanye nokunye ukuphuthuma kwezempilo yomphakathi. UDkt Ryan uyilungu elingumsunguli leGlobal Outbreak Alert and Response Network (GOARN) esisize ukuphendula kumakhulu okubheduka kwezifo emhlabeni wonke.
translations.reference: She joined WHO in 2021, having been the Chief Executive Officer of FIND, the international alliance for diagnostics. Dr Boehme holds a medical degree and diplomas in public health and management. Dr Chikwe Ihekweazu will continue as Assistant Director-General for the Division of Health Emergency Intelligence and Surveillance Systems in the Emergencies Programme. As part of his portfolio, Dr Ihekweazu leads the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence, bringing together partners to address future pandemic and epidemic risks with better access to data, analytical capacities, and tools for decision-making. Prior to joining WHO, Dr Ihekweazu was the first Director General of the Nigeria Centre for Disease Control (NCDC), from 2016 to 2021. Dr Ihekweazu holds a medical degree, with specialization as an infectious disease epidemiologist, and a master’s degree in public health. Dr Michael Ryan will continue as Executive Director of WHO’s Health Emergencies Programme. Dr Ryan has been managing health emergencies in WHO for the past 25 years and most recently led WHO’s emergency response to the COVID-19 pandemic. He leads WHO’s response to disease outbreaks, humanitarian crises and other public health emergencies. Dr Ryan is a founding member of the Global Outbreak Alert and Response Network (GOARN), which has aided the response to hundreds of disease outbreaks around the world.
